value.us_reopen: first US fixed-income and equity session after the three-day Juneteenth weekend (June 19 holiday; June 20-21 weekend)
value.comparison_basis: US daily moves measured vs Thursday 2026-06-18
value.dominant_driver: conclusion of first-round US-Iran talks in Switzerland with a 60-day roadmap, easing the Middle East risk premium

text: June 22, 2026 was a normal Monday global macro session and the first US fixed-income and equity session after the three-day Juneteenth weekend (June 19 holiday, June 20-21 weekend), so US rate and equity moves are measured against Thursday, June 18; the official Treasury par-yield CSV read live on June 23 has its newest row dated 06/22/2026 with no 06/19-06/21 row in between. The day's macro tape was set by the conclusion of the first round of US-Iran talks in Switzerland with a 60-day roadmap toward a final deal, which eased the Middle East risk premium, layered on a still-hawkish Fed backdrop.

calculation.method: read official Treasury par-yield CSV live; 10Y minus 2Y; change vs the 06/18/2026 row (no 06/19-06/21 row exists)

text: US Treasury par yields backed up across the curve on June 22, closing at 4.24% (2Y), 4.25% (3Y), 4.29% (5Y), 4.39% (7Y), 4.51% (10Y), 4.97% (20Y) and 4.95% (30Y) - a roughly +5 to +6 bp parallel rise from the June 18 pre-holiday close (2Y 4.19%, 10Y 4.46%, 30Y 4.90%) with the 2s10s slope unchanged at +27 bp, a bear shift rather than a steepening or flattening; the long end kept its small kink with the 20Y (4.97%) sitting 2 bp above the 30Y (4.95%). The yield back-up was the day's rates headwind for equities, weighing on the rate-sensitive Nasdaq.

value.high_basis: near highest since May 2025; fresh ~13-month high on June 23
value.policy_context: markets positioned for further Fed tightening, not cuts; Deutsche Bank and BofA Global Research both forecast a September rate hike after last week's hawkish Fed

calculation.method: vendor DXY daily close (June 22) with the June 23 spot as the carry cross-check

text: The U.S. Dollar Index closed June 22 around 101.0 and pushed to roughly 101.17 on June 23, a fresh high and its strongest since May 2025; the move is policy-driven, with May CPI back at 4.2%, the fed funds rate at 3.75%, markets positioned for further Fed tightening rather than cuts, and both Deutsche Bank and BofA Global Research now carrying a September hike after last week's hawkish Fed - this week's PCE print is the next catalyst. A firm dollar alongside rising yields is the tightening-leaning half of the June 22 macro picture.

value.reversal_driver: Vance signaled 'great progress' in the Switzerland talks and the Strait of Hormuz kept flowing, reversing an early spike driven by Trump's strike threat and Iran's Hormuz-closure claim
value.supply_leg: US Treasury issued a 60-day license authorizing production, delivery and sale of Iranian oil, including US imports payable in dollars
value.wti_contract_note: expiring July WTI rolled to the August contract

calculation.method: June 22 futures settlement prices; intraday path from same-day reporting

text: Crude fell hard on June 22: Brent settled down 3.3% at $77.90 and WTI lost 2.3% to settle at $74.82, both near three-month lows. Prices first spiked to about $82.30 early Monday on Trump's threat to restart strikes and Tehran's renewed Strait of Hormuz closure claim, then reversed as Vance signaled 'great progress' in the Switzerland talks and the strait kept flowing; the US Treasury's 60-day license authorizing Iranian-oil production, delivery and sale - including US imports payable in dollars - added a supply-bearish leg, with Brent now down roughly 20% over the past month.

value.outcome: first round concluded June 22 with a roadmap aimed at a final deal within 60 days, building on the June 17 memorandum of understanding
value.governance: High Level Committee for political oversight, with working groups on nuclear, sanctions and dispute resolution
value.lebanon: US-Iran-Lebanon de-confliction cell to end hostilities in Lebanon
value.hormuz: MoU reopens the Strait of Hormuz toll-free for at least 60 days
value.iran_claims_araghchi: waivers for oil and petrochemical exports, lifting of the port blockade, release of some frozen assets, and a reconstruction and development plan
value.continuation: technical talks to continue through the week

text: Mediators Qatar and Pakistan said the US and Iran, meeting at the Burgenstock resort above Lake Lucerne, agreed on June 22 on a roadmap aimed at a final deal within 60 days, building on the June 17 MoU; the parties created a High Level Committee for political oversight with working groups on nuclear, sanctions and dispute resolution, plus a US-Iran-Lebanon de-confliction cell, and set technical talks to continue through the week. The MoU reopens the Strait of Hormuz toll-free for at least 60 days, and Iran's FM Abbas Araghchi said Tehran secured waivers for oil and petrochemical exports, a lifted port blockade, the release of some frozen assets and a reconstruction plan - the mechanism that converted a war-risk premium into a supply-normalization trade.

value.talks_tone: constructive but tense, ran into the early hours
value.trump_threat: publicly threatened to hit Iran very hard again even as Vance negotiated
value.hormuz_dispute: Iran's IRGC declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again Saturday; US military denied it (Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz); Iran's Tasnim said the strait stays shut until a Lebanon ceasefire and oil-sanctions relief
value.lebanon: Israeli strikes reported to have killed at least 20 on Saturday (Lebanon's NNA); Araghchi called the Lebanon de-confliction mechanism the deal's first real test
value.analyst_caveats: ING flagged very real risks of a flare-up during the 60-day ceasefire; Quantum Strategy's David Roche warned the apparent oil abundance reflects inventory/tanker liquidation, not recovered production

text: The de-escalation is real but fragile, which is why the oil move was a partial repricing rather than a collapse: the talks were 'constructive but tense' and ran into the early hours, Trump publicly threatened to 'hit Iran very hard again' even as Vance negotiated, and Iran's IRGC had declared the Strait of Hormuz closed again Saturday - a claim the US military denied ('Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz'), with Iran's Tasnim saying the strait stays shut until a Lebanon ceasefire and oil-sanctions relief. Lebanon stayed the live flashpoint, with Israeli strikes reported to have killed at least 20 on Saturday and Araghchi calling the Lebanon de-confliction mechanism the deal's 'first real test'; ING flagged 'very real risks' of a flare-up and Quantum Strategy's David Roche warned the apparent oil abundance reflects inventory and tanker liquidation rather than recovered production. These items are evolving and carried at medium confidence.
